dateStatements.label: Manuscript production
dateStatements.notes: This dates the physical witness, not the c.1438 translation event.
dateStatements.value: Mid-fifteenth century; Zupitza proposed approximately the third quarter of the fifteenth century

entities.description: A parchment codex written in England in the mid-fifteenth century. It contains Lydgate's Siege of Thebes, Benedict Burgh's Cato, and Middle English lives of Alexius, Margaret, and Mary Magdalene. The target Life begins after the Margaret text on f.106r and runs through f.111v, where the final sentence breaks because the last leaf of quire 14 is missing.

holding.notes: Held in the Cosin Manuscripts at Durham University Library; the older Cathedral Library designation is retained only as a historical shelfmark.

entities.label: Durham University Library, Cosin MS V.ii.14

physicalDescription.extent: [1] + 3 preliminary leaves + 111 folios + 2 stiff leaves + [1] leaf; foliated i–iii, 1–111
physicalDescription.format: Codex; one hand in textura quadrata, 32 long lines
notes: One quire is missing at the beginning; quire 14 wants its eighth leaf after f.111.
George Davenport dated his ownership inscription 1664; he gave the manuscript to Bishop Cosin's Library around 1670.
The relevant leaves use red rubrication, red and blue initials, and colored paraphs visible in the digitized images.
The codex was microfilmed in 1985/86 by the Hill Monastic Manuscript Library; copies are reported at HMML and Durham.
physicalDescription.support: Parchment, with seventeenth-century paper among the preliminary leaves

next_steps: Consult Hamer and Russell's three-volume EETS Gilte Legende edition, especially its source and manuscript discussions.
Collate the Durham chapter's distinctive errors and additions against critical work on Jean de Vignay rather than selecting a convenient surviving French facsimile as the exemplar.
Retain the French relation at work level until a physical exemplar is evidenced.
openQuestions.question: Which French manuscript or textual family of Jean de Vignay's Légende dorée supplied the exemplar for the c.1438 Middle English Gilte Legende translation represented here?

next_steps: Audit all 191 English HI spans against ff.106r–111v at the highest image resolution permitted by Durham.
Record segment-level categories without treating German note italics or bracketed Latin corrections as the same phenomenon.
Preserve the existing italic signal until a fully evidenced normalization can distinguish expansion from supply and commentary.
openQuestions.question: Can every italic segment in Zupitza's Middle English transcription be classified exactly as an abbreviation expansion, supplied letter, damaged reading, or another editorial function?

next_steps: Ask Durham through its current contact form and search HMML Reading Room records by current and historical shelfmark.
Keep any supplied microfilm separate from the public color IIIF object and record its exact contractual terms.
openQuestions.question: What coverage, resolution, access procedure, and reuse terms apply to the 1985/86 HMML microfilm copies held by HMML and Durham?

reviewNotes: The intellectual work cataloged in the parallel metadata manifest is the incomplete Middle English Gilte Legende chapter, not Zupitza's whole article, its German introduction, or Grässe's Latin comparison text.
Zupitza's legacy shelfmark 5.2.14 resolves to current Durham University Library Cosin MS V.ii.14; it is not a separate Cathedral Library manuscript.

The current catalog HTML compresses the component extent to f.106r–v, but Zupitza's explicit f.106r–111v range, the manuscript's incomplete ending, and the digitized sequence establish the actual target extent as ff. 106r–111v.
The complete Durham digital object has 242 canvases from front cover through fore-edge. The work occupies canvases 221–232 and begins mid-page on f.106r after the preceding Life of Saint Margaret.
The last words on f.111v and in the English edition are 'anoyntid the bodi of hir with'; quire 14 lacks its eighth leaf after f.111, so the ending is physically lost.
The Latin stream in CME00108 is editorial comparison material from Grässe, not evidence that the cataloged medieval English work is intrinsically bilingual.
A representative surviving manuscript of Jean de Vignay's French tradition would not by itself be the Middle English translator's physical exemplar. No such exemplar is asserted here.
The ignored Durham bundle is a private research cache. Its CC BY-NC-ND 4.0 license does not authorize adaptation or commercial reuse in a profit-enabled print-on-demand workflow.

sourceLinks.entityLabel: Jean de Vignay, Vie de sainte Marie Madeleine in the Légende dorée
notes: These records identify the French source work and translation relationship; they are not facsimiles of the English translator's exemplar.

lineage.summary: The checked-in XML is a local corpus copy of University of Michigan item CME00108. U-M encoded only the bilingual text on printed pp. 210–224 of Julius Zupitza's 1893 article, omitting the article's pp. 207–209 introduction and reorganizing its page-by-page English/Latin parallel into separate continuous Middle English and Latin streams. Zupitza transcribed the incomplete Middle English Life of Saint Mary Magdalene from Durham University Library, Cosin MS V.ii.14, ff. 106r–111v, and printed beneath it the corresponding Latin from Theodor Grässe's 1890 edition of Jacobus de Voragine. Current Durham cataloging identifies the English text as the Gilte Legende translation from Jean de Vignay's French Légende dorée; the exact French exemplar remains unidentified.

catalogSummary.author: Anonymous Middle English translator
catalogSummary.date: Translation c.1438; surviving witness mid-fifteenth century; first known publication of this witness 1893
catalogSummary.region: England; exact translation place unknown; Zupitza describes the Durham text's language as East Midland
catalogSummary.title: Life of Saint Mary Magdalene (Gilte Legende, Durham fragment)

catalogingSubject.label: Life of Saint Mary Magdalene (Gilte Legende, Durham fragment)
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The cataloged subject is the surviving incomplete Middle English prose Life of Saint Mary Magdalene copied in Durham University Library Cosin MS V.ii.14, ff.106r–111v: a chapter of the anonymous c.1438 Gilte Legende translation from Jean de Vignay's French Légende dorée. It begins with its manuscript rubric and breaks in the final burial sentence because the following leaf is missing. The subject excludes the codex as a physical object, Julius Zupitza's 1893 article and German commentary, Theodor Grässe's Latin comparison text, the University of Michigan encoding, and the checked-in XML; those remain separately modeled lineage layers.

contentParts.label: Surviving Durham fragment of the Life of Saint Mary Magdalene
notes: The fragment starts mid-page on f.106r after the Life of Saint Margaret but at the beginning of its own text.
It is codicologically incomplete at the end: quire 14 wants the leaf following f.111.
The current Durham HTML's compressed f.106r–v component display is not used as the endpoint because its own explicit, Zupitza, and the digitized images establish continuation through f.111v.
contentParts.summary: The continuous surviving chapter from its rubric and opening genealogy through Mary's final communion and death to the incomplete beginning of Maximinus's burial preparations.

assertion.notes: The circa-1438 date belongs to the Gilte Legende translation project, not to the production of Cosin MS V.ii.14.

dateStatements.display: About 1438, as part of the anonymous Gilte Legende translation

assertion.notes: The current Durham catalog dates the codex to the mid-fifteenth century; Zupitza proposed approximately its third quarter.

dateStatements.display: Mid-fifteenth century in Durham University Library Cosin MS V.ii.14

assertion.notes: The publication date and witness identity are direct; 'first' is qualified because the inspected sources do not provide an exhaustive census of all earlier notices or excerpts.

dateStatements.display: 1893 (first known publication of the Durham witness, by Julius Zupitza)

extent.description: One incomplete Middle English prose saint's life on manuscript ff.106r–111v and printed pp.210–224. The English XML stream has one P element, one HEAD, 118 milestones, 22 editorial notes, 191 italic spans, and 14 descendant page breaks plus the opening p.210 page break outside DIV2. Zupitza's 107 comparison numbers are editorial alignment units, not an intrinsic medieval chapter count; the separate Latin P and its 114 milestones are source-edition apparatus rather than work extent.

formStatements.description: Continuous hagiographic prose, represented as one paragraph in the English XML stream and subdivided only by Zupitza's 107 comparison numbers.

assertion.notes: The immediate source is Jean de Vignay's French version; Jacobus's Latin work is upstream.

languageStatements.label: Middle English
notes: Latin in CME00108 is Grässe's editorial comparison text; German occurs in Zupitza's notes; French is the source-work language. None makes the cataloged Middle English fragment intrinsically multilingual.
The preferred title is supplied for discovery; the manuscript's own red rubric is separately preserved as an original title statement.
Anonymous Middle English translator is the authorship display. Julius Zupitza is editor and transcriber, not medieval author; Jean de Vignay and Jacobus de Voragine belong to source-work layers.
The c.1438 date describes the Gilte Legende translation, the mid-fifteenth-century date describes the Durham witness, and 1893 describes Zupitza's publication. These dates are not interchangeable.
The East Midland label is Zupitza's linguistic characterization, not a demonstrated place of translation or manuscript production.
Latin, German, and French are deliberately excluded from the work-level language facet: they belong respectively to Zupitza's comparison source, editorial commentary, and the upstream source work.
The missing final leaf makes the cataloged Durham text a fragment even though the underlying Gilte Legende chapter was originally complete.
The full Durham facsimile is cached privately and Git-ignored; the metadata points from the full codex to ff.106r–111v rather than caching a work-only crop.

next_steps: Consult Hamer and Russell's full EETS Gilte Legende edition and its source study.
Compare the Durham chapter's distinctive additions, omissions, and errors against critical work on Jean de Vignay.
Do not promote a merely representative surviving French manuscript to direct-source status without textual evidence.
openQuestions.question: Which French manuscript or textual family of Jean de Vignay's Légende dorée supplied the exemplar for the c.1438 English Gilte Legende translation represented here?

next_steps: Consult the EETS edition's linguistic introduction and current scholarship on Gilte Legende manuscripts.
Distinguish translator language, Durham scribal language, and place of manuscript production before adding a composition-place facet.
Retain East Midlands as a historical dialect claim with medium confidence until reassessed.
openQuestions.question: Can the anonymous Gilte Legende translator or the exact place of this chapter's translation be identified more narrowly than England, and does modern dialect analysis confirm Zupitza's East Midland characterization of the Durham text?

next_steps: Audit every span against ff.106r–111v at the best resolution Durham permits.
Separate abbreviation expansions, supplied letters, uncertain readings, and italic German commentary.
Preserve the edition's typography until segment-level evidence supports more precise markup.
openQuestions.question: Can all 191 italic spans in the English stream be assigned precise editorial functions from the manuscript images?

assertion.notes: The work is an English-language translation in an English manuscript; the exact place of translation remains unknown.
Zupitza says the language has a thoroughly East Midland character. This historical claim has not been silently converted into a place-of-composition or modern dialect-profile assertion.
The target is part of the anonymous Gilte Legende translation from Jean de Vignay's French version.

notes: Jacobus de Voragine is the author/compiler of the Latin source work and Jean de Vignay the French translator; neither is silently assigned direct authorship of this Middle English version.
This responsibility describes the 1893 edition represented by the CME item, not medieval intellectual authorship.
The German introduction itself is omitted from CME00108, while comparison markers and footnotes remain in the encoding.

scope.description: German textual introduction and the English/Latin comparison apparatus surrounding the edited fragment

summaries.value: The legend recounts Mary Magdalene's noble family, worldly life, repentance, intimacy with Christ, and place at the Resurrection. After the disciples' dispersion she travels with Lazarus, Martha, Maximinus, and others to Marseilles, preaches against idolatry, and converts a provincial ruler and his wife. Their pilgrimage to Saint Peter includes the wife's death in childbirth, miraculous preservation of mother and child, and restoration on the return voyage. Mary then converts communities, withdraws for thirty years into a desert retreat where angels raise and feed her, receives the Eucharist, and dies. The Durham copy breaks as Maximinus begins to anoint her body.

The surviving fragment runs continuously from the rubric and ancestry of Mary Magdalene through comparison unit 107. It includes the Gospel-derived penitent narrative, the legendary mission to Provence, the ruler's pilgrimage and family miracle, Mary's desert asceticism and angelic elevation, her final communion, and the beginning of her burial. A missing leaf after f.111 prevents completion of the burial and any remaining chapter ending.

The record treats the Middle English fragment as one work unit within the wider Gilte Legende rather than cataloging the whole bilingual 1893 article as a medieval work. Grässe's Latin is a comparison source selected by Zupitza, German is an editorial language, and Jean de Vignay's French and Jacobus's Latin are related source works. The manuscript, edition, digital encoding, and repository artifact are linked but not conflated.

assertion.notes: The qualifier distinguishes this incomplete Durham witness from the full Gilte Legende chapter and other medieval Mary Magdalene lives.

titles.value: Life of Saint Mary Magdalene (Gilte Legende, Durham fragment)

notes: The red rubric appears mid-page on f.106r after the preceding Life of Saint Margaret; terminal punctuation is editorial.
titles.value: Here bigynnyth the lyfe of Marye Magdaleyn
Gilte Legende: Life of Saint Mary Magdalene

notes: This is a modern German editorial title, not a medieval title or evidence that the work is German.

scope.description: Short form of Zupitza's editorial article title

titles.value: Das Leben der heiligen Maria Magdalena
Mary Magdaleyn was surnamed of Magdalon, the castell, and she was born of the kynred, that were descendid of ryal kynne

assertion.notes: This is the final surviving phrase, not the work's original complete explicit.

notes: The manuscript and edition break after with; the following leaf of quire 14 is missing.
titles.value: And seynt Maxymien anoyntid the bodi of hir with

After the disciples' dispersion she travels with Lazarus, Martha, Maximinus, and others to Marseilles, preaches against idolatry, and converts a provincial ruler and his wife. Their pilgrimage to Saint Peter includes the wife's death in childbirth, miraculous preservation of mother and child, and restoration on the return voyage. Mary then converts communities, withdraws for thirty years into a desert retreat where angels raise and feed her, receives the Eucharist, and dies.
